does: TrustErase introduces a verifiable machine unlearning framework that embeds cryptographic passports within neural network parameters using singular value decomposition. The method enables instant removal of specific classes or datasets by deactivating passport-embedded representations in parameter-efficient adaptation layers, without requiring model retraining, fine-tuning, or access to original training data. The system achieves unlearning through straightforward deactivation of learned modules associated with target data, while concealing passports within model weights to ensure transparency. Evaluations on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 demonstrate that TrustErase matches or exceeds performance of existing methods like DELETE, L2UL, and Boundary Shrink while operating entirely in a data-free setting, establishing auditability and provable compliance for forgetting operations.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research addressing privacy-compliant machine learning and the legal-technical challenge of machine unlearning required by regulations like GDPR. It extends prior approaches that use retraining or distillation-based methods by addressing their computational cost and lack of verifiability. TrustErase incorporates ideas from parameter-efficient adaptation techniques and modular network design, treating passports as cryptographic primitives similar to recent work embedding security properties in neural networks. The data-free unlearning requirement responds to practical scenarios where original training data is unavailable or expensive to retain, advancing beyond methods that require data access. The singular value decomposition-based concealment draws on matrix factorization techniques in neural network interpretability and security literature.
enables: This framework opens pathways toward trustworthy AI systems where forgetting can be audited and verified by regulators or third parties, establishing accountability mechanisms currently absent in unlearning research. The instant, modular approach suggests extensions to continual learning scenarios where models must selectively forget outdated or sensitive information while retaining general knowledge. The passport-embedding paradigm could inspire cryptographic integration in other neural network operations, supporting secure collaborative learning and privacy-preserving model updates. Future work might scale the approach to larger models and datasets, explore multi-user or adversarial unlearning scenarios, or integrate passport-based unlearning with federated learning systems. The framework also enables new business models where AI systems offer formal guarantees of data removal, potentially reshaping how organizations handle sensitive training data and liability concerns.
